DP700-001: Least-privilege workspace author

A data engineer must create and modify lakehouses, warehouses, notebooks, and pipelines in a Fabric workspace. The engineer must not add users, change workspace access, or update the workspace itself. Which workspace role should you assign?

  1. A. Admin
  2. B. Member
  3. C. Contributor
  4. D. Viewer
column2:
Correct answer: C. Contributor

Explanation:
The Contributor role can create and modify Fabric items and execute pipelines, but it cannot add or remove people or update the workspace. It therefore provides the required authoring capabilities without the access-management permissions excluded by the scenario.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Admin can update or delete the workspace and manage all workspace access, which exceeds the required permissions.
  • B. Member can add members and users with lower permissions, so it does not satisfy the access-management restriction.
  • C. Contributor supports item creation and modification without granting workspace or membership administration.
  • D. Viewer can read workspace content but cannot create or modify the required Fabric items.


Source: Roles in workspaces in Microsoft Fabric
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/get-started/roles-workspaces

column1:
DP700-002: Low-code cleansing for analysts

Business analysts need to remove duplicates, standardize column names, merge queries, and load curated data into a Fabric Warehouse. They prefer a visual Power Query experience and do not want to maintain code. Which Fabric item should form the transformation step?

  1. A. Dataflow Gen2
  2. B. Fabric notebook
  3. C. Eventstream
  4. D. Deployment pipeline
column2:
Correct answer: A. Dataflow Gen2

Explanation:
Dataflow Gen2 provides the visual, low-code Power Query experience described in the scenario. It supports cleansing, derived columns, aggregation, and query merging, and can write results to a Lakehouse or Warehouse.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Dataflow Gen2 directly meets both the low-code authoring and batch-transformation requirements.
  • B. A notebook is appropriate for advanced code-first transformations, but it conflicts with the analysts' no-code requirement.
  • C. Eventstream processes data in motion; the scenario calls for analyst-authored batch preparation with Power Query.
  • D. A deployment pipeline promotes Fabric content between lifecycle stages; it does not perform the requested data cleansing.


Source: Prepare and transform data in Microsoft Fabric
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/fundamentals/prepare-transform-data

column1:
DP700-003: Stage-specific notebook lakehouse

A notebook is promoted from development to test and then production by using a Fabric deployment pipeline. The same notebook code must run in every stage, but its default lakehouse must point to the lakehouse in the target stage after each deployment. What should you configure?

  1. A. A data source rule on the notebook
  2. B. A parameter rule on the notebook
  3. C. A default lakehouse deployment rule in each target stage
  4. D. A workspace Viewer role for the notebook owner
column2:
Correct answer: C. A default lakehouse deployment rule in each target stage

Explanation:
Fabric deployment pipelines support a default lakehouse rule specifically for notebooks. The rule is defined in the target stage and assigns that stage's lakehouse when content is deployed into it.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Deployment-pipeline data source rules are not supported for notebooks; notebooks use default lakehouse rules.
  • B. Parameter rules are not the supported rule type for assigning a notebook's default lakehouse.
  • C. This rule preserves the notebook while binding it to the correct target-stage lakehouse.
  • D. A workspace role controls a user's permissions and cannot change an item's stage-specific lakehouse binding.


Source: Create deployment rules for Fabric application lifecycle management
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/cicd/deployment-pipelines/create-rules

column1:
DP700-004: Version a Fabric SQL database schema

A team develops a SQL database in Fabric. It must track table, view, and stored-procedure definitions in Azure DevOps, review changes as code, and apply validated differential schema changes back to the live database. The team does not need to version table data. Which approach should it use?

  1. A. Connect the workspace to a Git repository and use the SQL database source-control integration
  2. B. Create a OneLake shortcut to the SQL database
  3. C. Schedule a Copy job to copy every database table to a lakehouse
  4. D. Use the Monitoring hub to retain the database definitions
column2:
Correct answer: A. Connect the workspace to a Git repository and use the SQL database source-control integration

Explanation:
Fabric SQL database Git integration converts the live database object definitions into code when committing and validates repository code before applying differential changes during an update. This matches the requirement to version schema rather than data.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Git integration provides the required schema history, code review workflow, validation, and differential update behavior.
  • B. A shortcut exposes existing data without copying it; it is not a database-object version-control mechanism.
  • C. A Copy job moves data and would duplicate table contents, but it does not version SQL object definitions.
  • D. Monitoring hub records workload runs; it is not a source repository for database schema.


Source: SQL database source control integration in Microsoft Fabric
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/database/sql/source-control

column1:
DP700-005: Restrict a lakehouse to one folder

OneLake security is enabled on a lakehouse. A Microsoft Entra group must read only the Files/curated/region-a folder across Fabric experiences. Its users currently inherit broad lakehouse read access through the automatically created DefaultReader role. Which action best enforces the requirement?

  1. A. Add the group to a new OneLake security role scoped to the folder and remove its broad access through DefaultReader or ReadAll
  2. B. Assign the group the workspace Contributor role and share the folder with Read permission
  3. C. Apply row-level security to every table and leave DefaultReader unchanged
  4. D. Create a shortcut to the folder and assign the group the workspace Viewer role
column2:
Correct answer: A. Add the group to a new OneLake security role scoped to the folder and remove its broad access through DefaultReader or ReadAll

Explanation:
OneLake security roles can grant access to specific folders. When the preview is enabled, existing readers can remain in DefaultReader through virtual membership based on ReadAll; that broader access must be removed or the folder-specific restriction is ineffective.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. This grants the required folder access and removes the existing path that exposes all lakehouse data.
  • B. Contributor grants broad workspace authoring and OneLake access, violating least privilege.
  • C. Row-level security applies to table rows, not an unstructured folder, and leaving DefaultReader preserves broad access.
  • D. A shortcut references data but does not override the source security model; Viewer is not a substitute for a folder-scoped OneLake role.


Source: Get started with OneLake security
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/onelake/security/get-started-data-access-roles

column1:
DP700-006: Reference ADLS data without copying

Several terabytes of Parquet files are already stored in an Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 account with hierarchical namespace enabled. A Fabric lakehouse must expose the current files quickly while avoiding another physical copy. What should you create?

  1. A. An ADLS Gen2 shortcut in the lakehouse
  2. B. A full-copy Copy job
  3. C. A Dataflow Gen2 destination that replaces the files
  4. D. A deployment pipeline stage
column2:
Correct answer: A. An ADLS Gen2 shortcut in the lakehouse

Explanation:
A OneLake shortcut can reference a folder in an ADLS Gen2 account from a lakehouse. It makes the external data accessible through Fabric without moving or duplicating the original files.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. The shortcut meets both the rapid-access and zero-copy requirements.
  • B. A full-copy job would create the additional physical copy the scenario prohibits.
  • C. Loading to a Dataflow Gen2 destination moves transformed data rather than exposing the existing files in place.
  • D. A deployment pipeline manages item lifecycle stages and does not provide access to external data.


Source: Create an Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 shortcut
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/onelake/create-adls-shortcut
column3: dp700 question-bank dp700-006 domain-ingest-and-transform-data difficulty-foundation topic-onelake-shortcuts

column1:
DP700-007: Incremental load that captures deletes

A supported source database has change data capture (CDC) enabled. A Fabric load must incrementally synchronize inserts, updates, and deletes to its destination and resume from the last successful state after a failure. Which configuration should you choose?

  1. A. An incremental Copy job that uses the detected CDC feed
  2. B. A watermark-based Copy job on an increasing integer column
  3. C. A full-copy Copy job that appends every source row on each run
  4. D. A OneLake shortcut to the relational table
column2:
Correct answer: A. An incremental Copy job that uses the detected CDC feed

Explanation:
For a supported CDC-enabled source, Copy job detects the change feed and transfers inserts, updates, and deletes since the last successful run. Copy job manages that successful-run state, so a failed run resumes without advancing the checkpoint.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. CDC satisfies the delete-capture requirement and provides managed incremental state.
  • B. A watermark can identify inserts and updates when its value changes, but it cannot detect source deletions.
  • C. Repeated full appends duplicate existing data and do not model source deletes.
  • D. A shortcut is a reference to supported file or OneLake locations, not a CDC synchronization process for this database source.


Source: Incremental copy in Copy job
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-factory/incremental-copy-job
column3: dp700 question-bank dp700-007 domain-ingest-and-transform-data difficulty-intermediate topic-incremental-loading-with-copy-job

column1:
DP700-008: Code-first custom transformation

A data engineering team must transform large semi-structured datasets in OneLake. The solution requires reusable Python functions, a third-party library, and custom PySpark logic before writing Delta tables. Which Fabric item is the best fit for the transformation?

  1. A. Dataflow Gen2
  2. B. Fabric notebook
  3. C. Deployment pipeline
  4. D. Activator
column2:
Correct answer: B. Fabric notebook

Explanation:
Fabric notebooks provide a code-first Spark environment for advanced transformations and support Python and PySpark. That makes a notebook the appropriate choice for reusable code, libraries, and custom processing of OneLake data.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Dataflow Gen2 is optimized for visual Power Query transformations and does not best meet the explicit custom PySpark and Python-library requirements.
  • B. A notebook provides the requested code-first language, library, and distributed Spark capabilities.
  • C. A deployment pipeline promotes items between environments; it does not execute the transformation logic described.
  • D. Activator detects conditions and triggers actions; it is not a batch data-transformation engine.


Source: Prepare and transform data in Microsoft Fabric
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/fundamentals/prepare-transform-data

column1:
DP700-009: No-code processing of events in motion

IoT telemetry arrives continuously from Event Hubs. Before storage, the solution must filter invalid events, remove duplicates, calculate five-minute windowed aggregates, and route different device categories to separate destinations. The team wants a no-code implementation. Which Fabric component should perform this processing?

  1. A. Eventstream
  2. B. Dataflow Gen2
  3. C. Deployment pipeline
  4. D. Warehouse SQL query editor
column2:
Correct answer: A. Eventstream

Explanation:
Eventstreams collect, transform, and route real-time data without code. Their event-processing capabilities include filtering, cleansing, windowed aggregation, duplicate detection, and content-based routing to destinations.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Eventstream natively combines the required no-code, streaming transformations and routing.
  • B. Dataflow Gen2 is a Power Query data-preparation tool and is not the component designed for continuous event-in-motion processing and routing.
  • C. A deployment pipeline manages application lifecycle promotion, not streaming records.
  • D. The Warehouse SQL query editor queries stored data; it does not provide this no-code event-routing topology before storage.


Source: What is Real-Time Intelligence in Microsoft Fabric?
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/overview

column1:
DP700-010: Recover a Structured Streaming job

A Spark Job Definition continuously writes Event Hubs data to a Delta table. After the job restarts, it must recover its streaming progress instead of beginning again with no record of prior work. Which configuration is essential on the streaming write?

  1. A. Set a persistent, job-specific checkpointLocation in the lakehouse
  2. B. Change outputMode to complete and omit a checkpoint
  3. C. Run VACUUM on the target before every microbatch
  4. D. Create a deployment rule for the target table
column2:
Correct answer: A. Set a persistent, job-specific checkpointLocation in the lakehouse

Explanation:
The Fabric Structured Streaming pattern stores checkpoint information in the lakehouse by setting checkpointLocation on writeStream. The checkpoint preserves the stream's progress and state so the job can recover across restarts.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. A durable checkpoint is the mechanism that records streaming progress for recovery.
  • B. Output mode controls how results are written; it does not replace durable checkpoint state.
  • C. VACUUM removes unreferenced Delta files and is unrelated to preserving streaming offsets or recovery state.
  • D. Deployment rules configure stage-specific item settings and do not checkpoint a running stream.


Source: Get streaming data into a lakehouse and access it with the SQL analytics endpoint
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/get-started-streaming

column1:
DP700-011: Central view of pipeline runs

An operator needs one centralized Fabric page to search, sort, and filter pipeline runs across Data Factory items by status, start time, run kind, and location. Where should the operator go?

  1. A. Monitoring hub
  2. B. OneLake catalog
  3. C. Deployment pipelines
  4. D. Manage connections and gateways
column2:
Correct answer: A. Monitoring hub

Explanation:
The Monitoring hub is the centralized portal for browsing pipeline runs across Fabric items. It supports searching and filtering runs and sorting columns such as status, start time, item type, location, and run kind.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Monitoring hub supplies the requested cross-item operational view and run filters.
  • B. OneLake catalog is used to discover data and Fabric items, not to inspect pipeline execution history.
  • C. Deployment pipelines move content through lifecycle stages rather than monitor Data Factory runs.
  • D. Connection and gateway settings manage connectivity but do not aggregate pipeline-run status.


Source: Browse pipeline runs in the Monitoring hub
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-factory/monitoring-hub-pipeline-runs

column1:
DP700-012: Isolate a failing pipeline activity

A newly authored Fabric pipeline has six activities, and one transformation is failing. You want to isolate the activity and validate its parameters without executing unrelated downstream work. What should you do?

  1. A. Deactivate the unrelated activities, save and run the pipeline, and inspect the result in Monitoring hub
  2. B. Start a separate authoring-time Debug run without saving the pipeline
  3. C. Delete the downstream activities and recreate them after testing
  4. D. Promote the pipeline to production and review the deployment comparison
column2:
Correct answer: A. Deactivate the unrelated activities, save and run the pipeline, and inspect the result in Monitoring hub

Explanation:
Fabric Data Factory has no separate authoring-time Debug mode. Its supported debugging workflow is to deactivate activities to isolate logic, save and run the pipeline, and then review the execution results in Monitoring hub.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Deactivation skips the isolated activities without destroying their definitions and supports incremental testing.
  • B. A separate unsaved Debug execution is an Azure Data Factory pattern, not the Fabric pipeline debugging model described in the documentation.
  • C. Deleting working activities is unnecessary and risks losing their configuration.
  • D. Deployment is a lifecycle-management action and should not be used to diagnose unvalidated logic in production.


Source: Debug pipelines in Microsoft Fabric
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-factory/how-to-debug-pipelines-in-microsoft-fabric

column1:
DP700-013: Alert on a live event threshold

A published eventstream carries machine telemetry. Operations staff need a Microsoft Teams notification whenever an individual event reports temperature > 90, and they want to manage activation history and test the notification rule. What should you configure?

  1. A. Use Set alert on the live eventstream and create an Activator rule with an On each event when condition
  2. B. Create a scheduled Dataflow Gen2 refresh that filters the temperature column
  3. C. Add a deployment rule that replaces the temperature parameter
  4. D. Run OPTIMIZE on the destination Delta table after each event
column2:
Correct answer: A. Use Set alert on the live eventstream and create an Activator rule with an On each event when condition

Explanation:
Setting an alert on a published, live eventstream automatically adds an Activator destination. The On each event when condition evaluates a field against a value, and Activator supports testing notifications and reviewing activation history.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. This directly evaluates each live event and provides the requested notification and rule-management features.
  • B. A scheduled batch refresh does not evaluate each event as it arrives and is not the documented eventstream alert path.
  • C. Deployment rules change stage-specific configuration during promotion; they do not monitor telemetry.
  • D. Delta optimization changes file layout and cannot send threshold-based notifications.


Source: Set an alert on an eventstream with an Activator destination
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/event-streams/set-alerts-event-stream

column1:
DP700-014: Compact and cluster a read-heavy Delta table

Frequent ingestion has left a read-heavy lakehouse Delta table with many small files. Reports repeatedly filter on OrderDate, and the table is scanned by multiple Fabric engines. Which Spark SQL maintenance action best addresses all three conditions in one operation?

  1. A. OPTIMIZE sales ZORDER BY (OrderDate) VORDER
  2. B. VACUUM sales RETAIN 168 HOURS
  3. C. UPDATE STATISTICS sales (OrderDate) WITH FULLSCAN
  4. D. DROP TABLE sales followed by a full reload
column2:
Correct answer: A. OPTIMIZE sales ZORDER BY (OrderDate) VORDER

Explanation:
OPTIMIZE bin-compacts small files, ZORDER BY (OrderDate) colocates values to improve data skipping for the recurring selective filter, and VORDER optimizes the Parquet layout for broad read efficiency across Fabric engines. The combined command targets every stated performance condition.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. It combines compaction, filter-column clustering, and cross-engine file-layout optimization in one supported Spark SQL operation.
  • B. VACUUM removes old unreferenced files; it does not compact current files or cluster current data for the filter.
  • C. Statistics can improve SQL plan estimates, but this action does not fix the Delta small-file layout or apply Z-Order and V-Order.
  • D. A destructive full reload is unnecessary and does not ensure the requested optimized physical layout.


Source: Optimize Delta Lake tables with V-Order in Fabric
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/delta-optimization-and-v-order

column1:
DP700-015: Refresh warehouse statistics after a major load

A large Fabric Warehouse load significantly changed the row count and distribution of CustomerKey in dbo.FactSales. Investigation shows that the existing user-defined statistic FactSales_CustomerKey is stale. An SLA-critical join will run next, so the statistic must be refreshed immediately from all rows. Which command should you use?

  1. A. UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.FactSales (FactSales_CustomerKey) WITH FULLSCAN
  2. B. DBCC SHOW_STATISTICS ('dbo.FactSales', 'FactSales_CustomerKey')
  3. C. VACUUM dbo.FactSales RETAIN 168 HOURS
  4. D. OPTIMIZE dbo.FactSales VORDER
column2:
Correct answer: A. UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.FactSales (FactSales_CustomerKey) WITH FULLSCAN

Explanation:
Accurate statistics help the Warehouse query optimizer estimate row counts and choose efficient plans. Microsoft recommends updating column statistics after data changes that materially alter row count or distribution; WITH FULLSCAN refreshes the named user-defined statistic from all table rows before the critical query.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. This explicitly refreshes the identified statistic with the complete current data distribution.
  • B. DBCC SHOW_STATISTICS displays the statistic and histogram but does not update either one.
  • C. VACUUM is Delta-file cleanup and does not refresh Warehouse optimizer statistics.
  • D. V-Order changes Parquet file layout for reads; it does not update the named histogram statistic.


Source: Statistics in Fabric Data Warehouse
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-warehouse/statistics

column1:
DP700-016: Query one lakehouse only through T-SQL

A Fabric workspace contains separate lakehouses for raw, refined, and curated data. A Microsoft Entra group of reporting analysts must run T-SQL queries against every Delta table in the curated lakehouse. The analysts must not receive access to the other workspace items or read the curated lakehouse through Apache Spark or OneLake APIs. Which access configuration should you use?

  1. A. Assign the group the workspace Viewer role
  2. B. Share the curated lakehouse with the group and grant Read all with SQL analytics endpoint
  3. C. Share the curated lakehouse with the group and grant Read all with Apache Spark
  4. D. Share only the curated lakehouse's default semantic model with the group and grant Build permission
column2:
Correct answer: B. Share the curated lakehouse with the group and grant Read all with SQL analytics endpoint

Explanation:
Sharing the curated lakehouse provides item-level access without making the analysts members of the workspace. The additional Read all with SQL analytics endpoint permission allows them to query all of its Delta tables through T-SQL without granting access through Apache Spark or OneLake APIs.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Viewer applies to every item in the workspace, so it exposes the raw and refined lakehouses as well as the curated lakehouse.
  • B. This combines item-level sharing with the required SQL-only data-access path and avoids workspace-wide access.
  • C. Read all with Apache Spark permits access through Spark and OneLake APIs, which the scenario explicitly prohibits.
  • D. Build permission supports creating content from a semantic model; it does not grant the required T-SQL access to every Delta table through the lakehouse's SQL analytics endpoint.


Source: Lakehouse sharing and permission management
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/lakehouse-sharing

column1:
DP700-017: Govern a shared Spark runtime

A data engineering team runs several Fabric notebooks and Spark job definitions. The team must use the same Spark runtime, libraries, and compute defaults for every new session, and workspace admins want those settings managed as one reusable workspace item. What should you configure?

  1. A. Create and publish a Fabric environment, then attach the notebooks and Spark job definitions to it
  2. B. Add every engineer to the workspace Admin role
  3. C. Store the library list in a lakehouse Files folder and import it from each notebook
  4. D. Create a Dataflow Gen2 item and schedule it before each notebook run
column2:
Correct answer: A. Create and publish a Fabric environment, then attach the notebooks and Spark job definitions to it

Explanation:
A Fabric environment is a workspace item for Spark session configuration. It can define the Spark runtime, compute settings, libraries, and small resources for notebooks and Spark job definitions. Publishing applies Spark compute and library changes, making the environment the reusable governed configuration the scenario requires.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. An environment directly centralizes the runtime, library, and Spark compute configuration for the affected Spark sessions.
  • B. Workspace Admin grants broad administration permissions but does not create reusable Spark runtime or library defaults for jobs.
  • C. A Files folder can store data or resources, but it does not govern Spark runtime selection, compute settings, or library publishing.
  • D. Dataflow Gen2 is a transformation item and cannot define Spark session settings for notebooks or Spark job definitions.


Source: Create, configure, and use an environment in Fabric
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/create-and-use-environment

column1:
DP700-018: Persist transformed data for Spark SQL

A PySpark notebook has transformed raw order rows into a dataframe named orders_curated_df. Analysts need to query the result later with Spark SQL, and the data should be stored as a managed Delta table in the lakehouse Tables area. Which code should you use?

  1. A. orders_curated_df.write.format("delta").saveAsTable("orders_curated")
  2. B. orders_curated_df.createOrReplaceTempView("orders_curated")
  3. C. orders_curated_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("Files/orders_curated")
  4. D. display(orders_curated_df)
column2:
Correct answer: A. orders_curated_df.write.format("delta").saveAsTable("orders_curated")

Explanation:
The Spark catalog can persist relational tables for Spark SQL queries. In Fabric, saving a dataframe with format("delta").saveAsTable(...) creates a managed table whose data is stored in the lakehouse Tables location. This satisfies the requirement for a later-queryable managed Delta table.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. This writes the dataframe as a Delta table in the Spark catalog, which makes it available for later Spark SQL queries.
  • B. A temporary view is removed at the end of the current Spark session, so it does not meet the persistence requirement.
  • C. Writing Parquet files under Files stores files, not a managed Spark catalog table in the Tables area.
  • D. display renders the dataframe in the notebook output but does not save data or create a queryable table.


Source: Work with data using Spark SQL
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/5-spark-sql
column3: dp700 question-bank dp700-018 domain-ingest-and-transform-data difficulty-foundation topic-spark-catalog-delta-tables

column1:
DP700-019: Average revenue by category

A Fabric notebook loads product sales into a PySpark dataframe named sales_df with columns Category and Revenue. You need a new dataframe that contains one row per category and the average revenue for that category. Which expression should you use?

  1. A. sales_df.groupBy("Category").agg({"Revenue": "avg"})
  2. B. sales_df.select("Category", "Revenue").where(sales_df["Revenue"] > 0)
  3. C. sales_df.agg({"Revenue": "avg"}).groupBy("Category")
  4. D. sales_df.write.partitionBy("Category").parquet("Files/sales_by_category")
column2:
Correct answer: A. sales_df.groupBy("Category").agg({"Revenue": "avg"})

Explanation:
PySpark DataFrame transformations use groupBy to group rows by a column and aggregation functions to calculate values for each group. Grouping by Category before applying the average aggregation returns a dataframe with one aggregated result per category.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. This groups rows by category and calculates the average revenue for each group.
  • B. This filters rows and keeps detail-level records; it does not aggregate revenue by category.
  • C. This attempts to aggregate the entire dataframe before grouping, so it does not produce category-level averages.
  • D. Partitioning output files by category changes storage layout but does not calculate an average value.


Source: Work with data in a Spark dataframe
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/4-dataframe

column1:
DP700-020: Accelerate supported Spark aggregations

A Fabric notebook repeatedly runs large aggregations and joins against Delta tables in OneLake. The code already uses supported Spark SQL and DataFrame operations, and the team wants acceleration without rewriting the transformations. The setting should apply to every new notebook session that uses the team's environment. What should you do?

  1. A. Enable the native execution engine in the environment's Acceleration settings, then save and publish the environment
  2. B. Convert the Delta tables to JSON files and keep the existing notebook code
  3. C. Disable adaptive query execution so every query uses a fixed physical plan
  4. D. Replace the notebook with a Dataflow Gen2 item to use Spark native execution automatically
column2:
Correct answer: A. Enable the native execution engine in the environment's Acceleration settings, then save and publish the environment

Explanation:
The Fabric native execution engine can accelerate supported Spark SQL and DataFrame workloads without code changes by offloading supported operators to a vectorized native execution path. Enabling it at the environment level and publishing the change lets subsequent notebooks and jobs attached to that environment inherit the setting.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. This applies the acceleration setting at the reusable environment level and matches the supported Delta aggregation and join scenario.
  • B. JSON queries are not the target acceleration path for the native execution engine, and converting from Delta would discard the stated table format advantage.
  • C. The native engine preserves Fabric Spark optimizations such as adaptive query execution; disabling it is not the documented enablement path.
  • D. Dataflow Gen2 is not the mechanism for enabling the Spark native execution engine for notebook sessions.


Source: Native execution engine for Fabric Data Engineering
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/native-execution-engine-overview

column1:
DP700-021: Visual cleanup before gold tables

A BI team receives weekly CSV extracts from a sales system. The team needs to remove duplicate rows, rename columns, merge lookup data, and load curated output to a gold table. The solution should use a visual Power Query experience and avoid custom code. Which Fabric item should you choose?

  1. A. Dataflow Gen2
  2. B. Fabric notebook
  3. C. Eventstream
  4. D. Eventhouse
column2:
Correct answer: A. Dataflow Gen2

Explanation:
Dataflow Gen2 is the Fabric low-code data preparation option that uses the Power Query experience. It supports visual transformations such as filters, derived columns, aggregations, and merges, and can write cleaned results to lakehouse or warehouse tables.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Dataflow Gen2 satisfies the low-code, Power Query, and batch data-cleaning requirements.
  • B. A notebook is better for code-first transformations and custom Spark logic, which the scenario explicitly avoids.
  • C. Eventstream is for real-time event ingestion, transformation, and routing rather than weekly file cleanup.
  • D. Eventhouse is optimized for storing and querying event-based telemetry and log data, not visual batch preparation.


Source: Prepare and transform data in Microsoft Fabric
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/fundamentals/prepare-transform-data

column1:
DP700-022: Orchestrate dependent Fabric tasks

A daily process must copy files from storage, run a Dataflow Gen2 transformation, execute a notebook only after the dataflow succeeds, and send a notification when the workflow completes. The team wants low-code control flow, scheduling, and monitoring for the full sequence. Which Fabric item should coordinate the process?

  1. A. Data pipeline
  2. B. Lakehouse
  3. C. Warehouse
  4. D. KQL database
column2:
Correct answer: A. Data pipeline

Explanation:
Data Factory pipelines orchestrate multiple data movement, transformation, and control-flow activities in one workflow. They can include activities such as Copy, Dataflow Gen2, notebooks, conditions, loops, schedules, and event-based triggers, which matches the required dependency chain.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. A data pipeline is designed to orchestrate activities, dependencies, schedules, and workflow monitoring.
  • B. A lakehouse stores and processes data, but it does not provide the requested activity sequencing and notification workflow by itself.
  • C. A warehouse supports SQL analytics and dimensional modeling, not general low-code orchestration across Fabric activities.
  • D. A KQL database stores and queries real-time analytics data; it is not the orchestration controller for batch workflows.


Source: What is Data Factory in Microsoft Fabric?
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-factory/data-factory-overview

column1:
DP700-023: Custom PySpark enrichment

A data engineering team must parse nested JSON files, call a Python package for address standardization, join the results to reference Delta tables, and write curated Delta tables back to OneLake. The transformation must be maintained as code. Which Fabric item is the best fit?

  1. A. Fabric notebook
  2. B. Dataflow Gen2
  3. C. Warehouse query editor
  4. D. Eventstream
column2:
Correct answer: A. Fabric notebook

Explanation:
Fabric notebooks provide a code-first Spark environment for Python, Scala, SQL, and R. They are suited for complex transformations, custom algorithms, external libraries, joins, windowed aggregations, and writing results back as Delta tables in OneLake.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. A notebook supports the required PySpark code, Python package usage, joins, and Delta output.
  • B. Dataflow Gen2 is optimized for visual low-code transformation and is not the best match for custom Python package logic.
  • C. The Warehouse query editor is SQL-focused and does not run the requested PySpark and Python-library transformation.
  • D. Eventstream processes events in motion and is not intended for this code-first batch enrichment.


Source: Prepare and transform data in Microsoft Fabric
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/fundamentals/prepare-transform-data

column1:
DP700-024: SQL-first dimensional model

An analytics team is building curated fact and dimension tables for enterprise reporting. The team needs SQL-first development, stored procedures, views, multi-table transactional behavior, and a governed semantic model for Power BI. Which Fabric item should host the model?

  1. A. Warehouse
  2. B. Eventstream
  3. C. Shortcut
  4. D. Dataflow Gen2
column2:
Correct answer: A. Warehouse

Explanation:
Fabric Data Warehouse is designed for enterprise-scale relational analytics on a lake foundation. It is primarily developed with T-SQL and is well suited for star and snowflake schemas, curated data marts, governed semantic models, stored procedures, views, and multi-table ACID transaction support.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Warehouse matches the SQL-first dimensional modeling, governed reporting, and transactional requirements.
  • B. Eventstream ingests and routes streaming events; it does not host a dimensional warehouse model.
  • C. A shortcut references existing data without copying it, but it is not a SQL-first modeling item.
  • D. Dataflow Gen2 prepares data visually, but it does not provide the relational warehouse surface requested.


Source: What is Fabric Data Warehouse?
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-warehouse/data-warehousing

column1:
DP700-025: Batch files and Delta tables

A data engineering team must land raw Parquet and CSV files, keep unstructured files available, transform data with Spark, and publish curated Delta tables for SQL analytics without moving data between separate systems. Which Fabric item should be the primary storage and engineering workspace?

  1. A. Lakehouse
  2. B. Warehouse
  3. C. Eventhouse
  4. D. Data pipeline
column2:
Correct answer: A. Lakehouse

Explanation:
A Fabric lakehouse combines data lake storage with warehouse-style querying. It stores structured and unstructured data in OneLake, manages tables with Delta Lake, and supports both Apache Spark and SQL access without moving data between systems.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Lakehouse supports files, Delta tables, Spark transformations, and SQL analytics over one copy of data.
  • B. Warehouse is better for SQL-first structured dimensional models, not mixed file storage and Spark-first engineering.
  • C. Eventhouse is built for event-based telemetry, logs, and KQL analytics rather than general batch file and Delta table engineering.
  • D. A data pipeline orchestrates work but is not the storage item for files and Delta tables.


Source: What is a lakehouse in Microsoft Fabric?
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/data-engineering/lakehouse-overview

column1:
DP700-026: Analyze high-volume telemetry

A platform team must store and analyze billions of application log events, JSON telemetry records, and time-series metrics. Users need fast KQL exploration over structured, semistructured, and free-text data with near-real-time ingestion. Which Fabric item should you use?

  1. A. Eventhouse with a KQL database
  2. B. Warehouse
  3. C. Dataflow Gen2
  4. D. Shortcut
column2:
Correct answer: A. Eventhouse with a KQL database

Explanation:
Eventhouses are designed for storing and analyzing streaming event data and can query billions of events quickly. They are tailored to time-based events, telemetry, logs, structured records, semistructured data such as JSON, and unstructured free-text analysis through KQL databases.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Eventhouse with a KQL database directly matches high-volume event, telemetry, log, and KQL analytics requirements.
  • B. Warehouse is SQL-first and suited to relational analytics models, not KQL-first streaming telemetry exploration.
  • C. Dataflow Gen2 is for visual data preparation, not high-volume event storage and KQL querying.
  • D. A shortcut references data in place but does not provide the event-optimized query and storage engine.


Source: Eventhouse overview
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/eventhouse

column1:
DP700-027: Expose data without duplication

A lakehouse already contains governed Delta tables used by another team. Your team must expose a subset of those tables in its own lakehouse for analysis, but storage policy prohibits making another physical copy. Which Fabric feature should you create?

  1. A. OneLake shortcut
  2. B. Full-copy data pipeline
  3. C. Warehouse table created with CTAS
  4. D. Dataflow Gen2 destination table
column2:
Correct answer: A. OneLake shortcut

Explanation:
OneLake shortcuts let a lakehouse or KQL database reference existing OneLake data from another location. The shortcut appears in the target item while pointing to the source data, which avoids duplicating the underlying files or tables.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. A shortcut exposes existing data in place and satisfies the no-copy requirement.
  • B. A full-copy pipeline creates the extra physical copy that the scenario prohibits.
  • C. Creating a warehouse table with CTAS materializes another table rather than referencing the source data in place.
  • D. A Dataflow Gen2 destination writes prepared output and would create another stored copy.


Source: Create and manage a OneLake shortcut
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/onelake/create-onelake-shortcut

column1:
DP700-028: Capture streaming source events

A manufacturer wants to ingest live IoT Hub messages into Fabric, apply lightweight transformations, and route the stream to multiple destinations for storage and analytics. The team wants a no-code streaming ingestion experience with built-in source connectors. Which Fabric item should it create?

  1. A. Eventstream
  2. B. Warehouse
  3. C. Lakehouse shortcut
  4. D. Dataflow Gen2
column2:
Correct answer: A. Eventstream

Explanation:
Fabric eventstreams bring real-time events into Fabric, can transform event data, and route the stream to supported destinations without writing code. Eventstreams include source connectors such as Azure Event Hubs and Azure IoT Hub for streaming ingestion.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Eventstream provides the required no-code streaming ingestion, transformation, source connectors, and routing.
  • B. Warehouse is for SQL analytics over structured data and is not the streaming ingestion component.
  • C. A lakehouse shortcut references existing data without copying it; it does not ingest live events from IoT Hub.
  • D. Dataflow Gen2 is a low-code batch preparation tool, not the event ingestion and routing service for live streams.


Source: Overview of Microsoft Fabric eventstreams
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/event-streams/overview

column1:
DP700-029: Connect an existing streaming source

A data engineering team already has a streaming source available in Real-Time Hub. The team creates a Fabric eventstream and needs to add that source to the eventstream without defining a brand-new source connection. Which action should the team take?

  1. A. Use the eventstream canvas to connect to the existing source from Real-Time Hub
  2. B. Create a OneLake shortcut to the Real-Time Hub source
  3. C. Create a Lakehouse destination before adding the source
  4. D. Use Fabric Activator to create the eventstream source
column2:
Correct answer: A. Use the eventstream canvas to connect to the existing source from Real-Time Hub

Explanation:
After an eventstream is created, sources are added through the eventstream canvas. The unit states that you can either create a new source or connect to an existing source from Real-Time Hub, which directly matches the scenario.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. This uses the supported source configuration path and reuses the already available Real-Time Hub source.
  • B. A OneLake shortcut references existing data locations; it is not the mechanism for attaching a streaming source to an eventstream.
  • C. A destination receives processed event data after a source, and optional transformations, have been configured. It does not attach the existing source.
  • D. Fabric Activator is a destination used to detect conditions and trigger actions; it does not create eventstream source connections.


Source: Eventstream sources and destinations
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/explore-event-streams-microsoft-fabric/3-setup-eventstreams
column3: dp700 question-bank dp700-029 domain-ingest-and-transform-data difficulty-foundation topic-configuring-eventstream-sources

column1:
DP700-030: Route event subsets by content

An eventstream receives IoT sensor events from a factory. High-temperature events must be routed to Fabric Activator for alerting, while hourly aggregate events must be routed to a KQL database for analysis. The team wants the routing to be based on event content without changing the original stream for other consumers. Which eventstream destination pattern should it use?

  1. A. Create derived streams from the original stream and route each derived stream to the appropriate destination
  2. B. Send every event only to a Lakehouse destination and create alerts from the Delta table later
  3. C. Replace the eventstream with a Dataflow Gen2 item that filters the IoT events
  4. D. Create a custom endpoint and require downstream systems to split the event content
column2:
Correct answer: A. Create derived streams from the original stream and route each derived stream to the appropriate destination

Explanation:
Derived streams are transformed versions of the original data stream and support content-based routing. The unit gives the pattern of filtering IoT sensor data so high-temperature events can go to Fabric Activator while hourly averages go to a KQL database.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. Derived streams preserve the content-based routing requirement and allow different destinations for different processed subsets.
  • B. Sending everything only to a Lakehouse delays the immediate alerting requirement and does not use the eventstream routing pattern described.
  • C. Dataflow Gen2 is not the eventstream destination pattern for routing real-time stream subsets to Activator and KQL.
  • D. A custom endpoint can send events to an external application, but it shifts the routing work out of Fabric and does not satisfy the requested in-eventstream content-based routing.


Source: Eventstream sources and destinations
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/explore-event-streams-microsoft-fabric/3-setup-eventstreams

column1:
DP700-031: Use multiple destinations after a transformation

A city mobility team uses an eventstream to group bicycle availability events by street. The transformed output must be inserted into an Eventhouse table for KQL analysis and must also drive an Activator rule that checks whether stations have available bikes. Both consumers need the same transformed output, and neither path should block or replace the other. What should the team configure?

  1. A. Attach both an Eventhouse destination and a Fabric Activator destination after the transformation
  2. B. Create only an Eventhouse destination because an eventstream can have only one active destination
  3. C. Route the stream to a custom endpoint and then manually reload the data into Eventhouse and Activator
  4. D. Store the events in a Lakehouse first because Activator cannot consume eventstream output
column2:
Correct answer: A. Attach both an Eventhouse destination and a Fabric Activator destination after the transformation

Explanation:
Eventstream destinations can be configured after a data source is connected or after optional transformations are applied. The unit states that multiple destinations can be attached to the same eventstream at the same time without colliding, and its example routes transformed GroupByStreet output to both Activator and Eventhouse.

Choice reasoning:
  • A. This keeps the transformed output inside the eventstream and sends it to both required destinations independently.
  • B. The unit explicitly supports multiple destinations in an eventstream, so limiting the flow to one destination is unnecessary.
  • C. A custom endpoint is useful for external systems, but it adds avoidable manual routing and does not use the built-in Eventhouse and Activator destinations.
  • D. Eventstreams can connect directly to Fabric Activator as a destination, so a Lakehouse staging step is not required for the alerting path.


Source: Eventstream sources and destinations
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/explore-event-streams-microsoft-fabric/3-setup-eventstreams

column1: PDF concept: In Microsoft Fabric, what storage format does OneLake use by default?
column2:
OneLake uses Delta-Parquet, which combines Parquet storage with Delta Lake table capabilities for lakehouse analytics.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 4.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/introduction-end-analytics-use-microsoft-fabric/5-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: Which Fabric experience is the best match for moving and transforming data through low-code pipelines?
column2:
Use Data Factory when the requirement is to move, orchestrate, and transform data with pipeline and dataflow-style capabilities.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 4.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/introduction-end-analytics-use-microsoft-fabric/5-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: Why does OneLake help Fabric AI features such as Copilot and data agents?
column2:
The unified storage model lets AI features work over the same governed data without requiring separate copies or preparation pipelines for each tool.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 4.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/introduction-end-analytics-use-microsoft-fabric/5-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: In a Fabric notebook, why define an explicit schema when reading CSV files into a Spark DataFrame?
column2:
An explicit schema gives columns meaningful names and data types instead of relying on generic names or inferred types that may not match the intended analytics model.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: How can a Fabric Spark read load all CSV files from an orders folder in one operation?
column2:
Use a wildcard path such as Files/orders/*.csv with spark.read.format("csv").schema(...).load(...) so every matching file is read into the DataFrame.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: What happens to the original DataFrame when you create a filtered or selected DataFrame in Spark?
column2:
DataFrame transformations return a new DataFrame. The original DataFrame is not modified by operations such as select, where, or chained transformations.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: Which DataFrame pattern should you use to return only customer name and email rows for one product?
column2:
Select the needed columns and then filter the rows, for example df.select("CustomerName", "Email").where(df["Item"] == "Road-250 Red, 52").

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: What does df.select("Item", "Quantity").groupBy("Item").sum() produce?
column2:
It groups rows by product item and returns aggregate sums for the numeric selected columns, such as total quantity by item.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: How do you derive a year column from OrderDate before grouping Spark DataFrame rows?
column2:
Import Spark SQL functions, use year(col("OrderDate")).alias("Year"), then group by the derived column and aggregate or count as needed.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: Which Spark functions are useful for splitting a full customer name into first and last name columns?
column2:
Use withColumn with split(col("CustomerName"), " ") and getItem(...) to derive the separate name columns.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: When should you save transformed lakehouse data as Parquet files under Files?
column2:
Use Parquet files when the requirement is efficient file storage or file-based downstream processing, not a managed Spark catalog table.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: Why partition large Parquet outputs by columns such as Year and Month?
column2:
Partitioning organizes files into folder paths by partition values, which can reduce the amount of data scanned for filters that target those values.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: What code pattern saves a Spark DataFrame as a managed Delta table in the Fabric lakehouse Tables area?
column2:
Use df.write.format("delta").saveAsTable("table_name"). The table is registered in the Spark catalog and stored as a managed Delta table.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: What does the %%sql magic do in a Fabric notebook cell?
column2:
It runs the cell as Spark SQL instead of PySpark, allowing direct SQL queries against catalog objects such as lakehouse Delta tables.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: When should you use built-in Fabric notebook charts instead of Python plotting libraries?
column2:
Use built-in charts for quick visual checks in notebook results. Use libraries such as matplotlib or seaborn when you need more control over chart layout and styling.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: Why might Spark notebook visualization code convert a Spark DataFrame to a Pandas DataFrame?
column2:
Libraries such as matplotlib and seaborn operate on local Python/Pandas data structures, so charting often requires converting the Spark result after reducing it to a manageable size.

Source: files/Analyze data with Apache Spark in Fabric _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/02-analyze-spark.html

column1: PDF concept: What is the Spark catalog used for?
column2:
The Spark catalog is a metastore for relational objects such as views and tables, letting Spark languages and Spark SQL query the same registered objects.

Source: files/Work with data using Spark SQL - Training _ Microsoft Learn.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/5-spark-sql

column1: PDF concept: What is the key difference between a Spark temporary view and a persisted Spark table?
column2:
A temporary view is removed when the Spark session ends. A persisted table remains in the catalog and can be queried later.

Source: files/Work with data using Spark SQL - Training _ Microsoft Learn.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/5-spark-sql

column1: PDF concept: What is the difference between a managed Spark table and an external Spark table in Fabric?
column2:
A managed table stores its underlying data in the managed Tables location, and deleting it deletes that data. An external table stores metadata in the catalog but points to data in an external location, so deleting the table does not delete the files.

Source: files/Work with data using Spark SQL - Training _ Microsoft Learn.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/5-spark-sql

column1: PDF concept: Why is Delta preferred for Spark catalog tables in Microsoft Fabric?
column2:
Delta tables add relational-style capabilities such as transactions, versioning, and streaming support on top of lakehouse storage.

Source: files/Work with data using Spark SQL - Training _ Microsoft Learn.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/5-spark-sql

column1: PDF concept: What Spark pool setting helps handle varying data volumes efficiently?
column2:
Autoscale lets the Spark pool adjust the number of nodes based on workload size instead of forcing a fixed node count for every job.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 1.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/8-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: Where do you choose the Apache Spark runtime version for Fabric tasks that use a custom environment?
column2:
Specify the Spark runtime in the Fabric environment settings so notebooks and jobs that use that environment run on the intended runtime version.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/8-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: What is the purpose of a default Spark pool in a Fabric workspace?
column2:
It gives Spark jobs an automatically selected pool when the user or job does not specify a particular Spark pool.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 2.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/8-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: Which Fabric Spark setting directly targets query execution performance with vectorized operations?
column2:
Enable the native execution engine through the relevant Spark environment properties when the workload and runtime support it.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 1.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/8-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: What is an Eventhouse in Microsoft Fabric used for?
column2:
An eventhouse stores and analyzes real-time event data, often ingested from eventstreams, in one or more KQL databases.

Source: files/Work with data in a Microsoft Fabric eventhouse _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/12-query-data-in-kql-database.html

column1: PDF concept: What does the pipe character separate in a KQL tabular expression?
column2:
It separates query operators so each step consumes the tabular output from the previous step, for example table input followed by project, where, or summarize.

Source: files/Work with data in a Microsoft Fabric eventhouse _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/12-query-data-in-kql-database.html

column1: PDF concept: In KQL, when should you use project?
column2:
Use project to select, rename, or compute the columns returned by a query, which keeps the result focused on the attributes needed for analysis.

Source: files/Work with data in a Microsoft Fabric eventhouse _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/12-query-data-in-kql-database.html

column1: PDF concept: In KQL, what is the role of summarize?
column2:
summarize performs aggregations, optionally grouped by one or more columns or expressions, such as total bikes by neighborhood.

Source: files/Work with data in a Microsoft Fabric eventhouse _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/12-query-data-in-kql-database.html

column1: PDF concept: How can KQL group null or empty dimension values into a readable fallback category?
column2:
Use case with checks such as isempty(...) and isnull(...) to replace missing values with a label such as Unidentified.

Source: files/Work with data in a Microsoft Fabric eventhouse _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/12-query-data-in-kql-database.html

column1: PDF concept: Which KQL operators can sort query results?
column2:
Use sort by or order by. In the Fabric KQL examples, they behave the same for ordering result rows.

Source: files/Work with data in a Microsoft Fabric eventhouse _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/12-query-data-in-kql-database.html

column1: PDF concept: Which KQL operator filters rows based on a condition?
column2:
Use where, combining conditions with logical operators such as and and or when needed.

Source: files/Work with data in a Microsoft Fabric eventhouse _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/12-query-data-in-kql-database.html

column1: PDF concept: Why is KQL usually preferred over the T-SQL endpoint for KQL databases?
column2:
The T-SQL endpoint exists for compatibility and has limitations. KQL is the native query language for KQL databases and generally offers fuller capability and better fit for event analytics.

Source: files/Work with data in a Microsoft Fabric eventhouse _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/12-query-data-in-kql-database.html

column1: PDF concept: What operation types are not supported by the T-SQL endpoint for a KQL database?
column2:
The endpoint is for querying and does not support table DDL or data modification operations such as creating, altering, dropping, inserting, updating, or deleting data.

Source: files/Work with data in a Microsoft Fabric eventhouse _ mslearn-fabric.pdf
Link: https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-fabric/Instructions/Labs/12-query-data-in-kql-database.html

column1: PDF concept: Why apply a time-based filter early in a KQL query over time-series data?
column2:
Filtering early reduces the amount of data that later operators must process, which is especially important for high-volume time-series tables.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 3.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/query-data-kql-database-microsoft-fabric/6-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: How do materialized views improve KQL database query performance?
column2:
They maintain precomputed aggregations or results so repeated analytical queries can read prepared data instead of recalculating from the full source each time.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 3.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/query-data-kql-database-microsoft-fabric/6-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: What is the purpose of stored functions in KQL analysis?
column2:
Stored functions encapsulate reusable query logic so repeated calculations stay consistent across analysts, dashboards, and operational queries.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 3.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/query-data-kql-database-microsoft-fabric/6-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: Which feature lets a KQL database in an eventhouse query data from Azure Data Explorer without copying it into the database?
column2:
Use a database shortcut when the goal is to reference an Azure Data Explorer database from the eventhouse KQL database.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 3.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/query-data-kql-database-microsoft-fabric/6-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: Why place the smaller table first when designing some KQL joins?
column2:
Putting the smaller side first can reduce rows processed during the join and improve query efficiency.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 3.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/query-data-kql-database-microsoft-fabric/6-knowledge-check

column1: PDF concept: Which KQL operator combines rows from two tables by matching key values?
column2:
Use join. In KQL, the join operator merges rows from two tabular inputs by matching values in the specified columns.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 5.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/kusto/query/join-operator?view=microsoft-fabric

column1: PDF concept: Which Eventstream transformation combines data from two streams using a matching condition?
column2:
Use the Join transformation. In Fabric eventstreams, Join combines data from two streams when the configured matching condition is met.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 5.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/event-streams/process-events-using-event-processor-editor

column1: PDF concept: What should you verify when a Real-Time Dashboard tile is not showing current event data?
column2:
Check the dashboard data source and refresh configuration. Real-Time Dashboard tiles query connected sources such as KQL databases, and live refresh must be enabled when the visual should update as new data arrives.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 5.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/dashboard-real-time-create

column1: PDF concept: What Activator configuration is required before accurate streaming data can trigger automated actions?
column2:
Define and activate rules that evaluate the relevant object properties and specify the action to run. Without rules, Activator has no condition/action logic to execute.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 5.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/data-activator/activator-introduction

column1: PDF concept: Why map event fields to object properties in Fabric Activator?
column2:
Activator rules evaluate object properties. Mapping the event fields to the correct properties ensures the rule is checking the intended values for each monitored object.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 5.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/data-activator/activator-introduction

column1: PDF concept: How should Activator be configured to respond automatically to real-time anomaly thresholds?
column2:
Create rules over the relevant metrics or properties, set threshold or pattern conditions, and attach actions such as notifications, pipelines, notebooks, or Power Automate flows.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 5.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/data-activator/activator-introduction

column1: PDF concept: Which Real-Time Intelligence component should trigger rerouting or corrective workflows when a live traffic pattern is detected?
column2:
Use Fabric Activator. Eventstreams can ingest and route events, and dashboards can visualize data, but Activator evaluates conditions and triggers actions when patterns or thresholds are detected.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 5.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/data-activator/activator-introduction

column1: PDF concept: What are the three main component types in a Fabric eventstream?
column2:
An eventstream is built from sources, optional transformations, and destinations. Sources bring events in, transformations process the events while they flow, and destinations receive the events for storage, analysis, alerts, or downstream integration.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 8.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/event-streams/overview

column1: PDF concept: What is the role of a source in a Fabric eventstream?
column2:
A source is where the event data enters the eventstream. Fabric eventstreams can ingest from Microsoft services and non-Microsoft platforms, including connectors such as Event Hubs, IoT Hub, Kafka-style sources, sample data, and change-data-capture sources.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 8.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/event-streams/overview

column1: PDF concept: Why add transformations to an eventstream before routing events to a destination?
column2:
Transformations let you reshape event data while it is flowing, such as filtering events, managing fields, aggregating or grouping values over time windows, expanding arrays, joining streams, or using SQL-based processing.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 8.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/event-streams/process-events-using-event-processor-editor

column1: PDF concept: What is the role of a destination in a Fabric eventstream?
column2:
A destination is where the eventstream sends events after optional processing. Destinations can support storage, analysis, alerts, or integration, such as Eventhouse or KQL database, Lakehouse, custom endpoint, derived stream, Spark notebook, or Fabric Activator.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 8.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/event-streams/overview

column1: PDF concept: What does the Fabric eventstream canvas help you design without code?
column2:
The eventstream canvas is a visual editor for building the streaming pipeline by arranging source, transformation, and destination nodes. It lets you design and inspect event flow without writing code or managing streaming infrastructure.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 8.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/event-streams/overview

column1: PDF concept: In the bicycle eventstream example, what does the GroupByStreet transformation prepare for storage?
column2:
It aggregates the bicycle events by street, such as summing bike counts by station street name, before routing the transformed results to an Eventhouse table for later KQL analysis.

Source: files/Module assessment - Training _ Microsoft Learn 8.pdf
Link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/real-time-intelligence/event-streams/process-events-using-event-processor-editor

column1: Consolidated review: You need to calculate the average SalesAmount for each Category in a Spark DataFrame. Which code pattern should you use?
column2:
Use df.groupBy('Category').agg({'SalesAmount': 'avg'}).

Why: groupBy creates one group per category, and agg calculates the average per group.

column1: Consolidated review: What is a best practice when configuring Spark pools in Microsoft Fabric to handle varying data volumes efficiently?
column2:
Enable autoscale so the pool can adjust nodes based on workload/data volume.

Why: a static node count can under-provision large jobs or waste resources on small jobs.


Source link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/8-knowledge-check

column1: Consolidated review: A team needs the fastest possible execution for complex PySpark calculations on a large dataset. Which Spark pool configuration best fits?
column2:
Enable the native execution engine and use memory-optimized nodes.

Why: the native execution engine accelerates supported operations, and memory-optimized nodes help large computations with heavy in-memory processing.


Source link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/8-knowledge-check

Source file: files/DP-700 Consolidated Review.txt

column1: Consolidated review: When creating a custom environment in Microsoft Fabric, which setting lets you use a specific Apache Spark version?
column2:
Specify the Spark runtime in the environment settings.

Why: the Spark runtime controls the Apache Spark version used by notebooks/jobs attached to that environment.


Source link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/8-knowledge-check

column1: Consolidated review: Which configuration setting can directly impact Spark SQL query performance in Microsoft Fabric?
column2:
Enabling the native execution engine for vectorized operations.

Why: it is a performance-related execution setting; chart color and font size do not affect query execution.

column1: Consolidated review: Which Spark DataFrame method is most efficient for finding the maximum value in a numerical column?

Use agg, for example df.agg({'ColumnName': 'max'}).

Why: agg performs an aggregate calculation; groupBy groups rows and filter only selects rows.

column1: Consolidated review: Which magic command executes SQL queries directly in a Microsoft Fabric notebook cell?

column2:
A new DataFrame with one row per Category and the sum of SalesAmount for each category.

Use df.write.format('delta').saveAsTable('table_name').

column1: Consolidated review: A DataFrame has a string column named Date and you need to convert it to a date type. Which PySpark method is appropriate?

Use withColumn, typically with a conversion function such as to_date.

column1: Consolidated review: Why does df.select('ProductID','Category').groupBy('Category').count() not include ProductID in the output?
column2:
Because after groupBy('Category'), Spark returns only the grouping column(s) and aggregate result(s), such as count. ProductID is not grouped or aggregated, so it is removed.

column1: Consolidated review: What is the Spark catalog?
column2:
A metastore for relational data objects such as views and tables. Spark can use it to integrate Spark code with SQL expressions.

column1: Consolidated review: How can you make a Spark DataFrame queryable through the Spark catalog only for the current session?
column2:

Temporary views are automatically deleted at the end of the current Spark session.

column1: Consolidated review: What happens when you delete a managed Spark table in Microsoft Fabric?

column1: Consolidated review: What happens when you delete an external Spark table?
column2:
Only the table metadata is deleted; the underlying external data is not deleted.

Why: external tables point to data in an external storage location, often the Files area of a lakehouse.


Source link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/modules/use-apache-spark-work-files-lakehouse/5-spark-sql

column1: Consolidated review: What is the preferred table format in Microsoft Fabric Spark catalog tables?

Delta.

column1: Consolidated review: How do you query a Spark catalog table from PySpark using SQL?

Use spark.sql('SELECT ... FROM table_name'), which returns a DataFrame.

column1: Consolidated review: How can partitioning files by Year and Month improve performance in Spark?

It lets Spark skip irrelevant partition folders when filtering by those columns, reducing the amount of data read.

It can create too many small files and folders, worsening the small-file problem and adding overhead.

Kusto Query Language (KQL).

column1: Consolidated review: Which KQL operator selects specific columns?

| take 10.

Use where.

Data Factory.

column1: Consolidated review: What is the small-file problem in Spark/Delta workloads?
